coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of transparent bistable switching memory device using plasmapolymerized hexamethyldisiloxane layers with embedded graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ooi, Poh Choon
Wee, M.F.Mohd. Razip
Dee, Chang Fu
Yap, Chi Chin
Salleh, Muhammad Mat
Majlis, Burhanuddin Yeop
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrated the feasibility to fabricate two-terminal non-volatile-memory (NVM) devices using pulsed radio frequency (rf) plasma polymerization and simple solution route. The two-terminal NVM devices were fabricated based on a metal-insulator-metal structure consisting of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) embedded in hexamethyldisiloxane dielectric layers. The charge trapping layer, GQDs and the top contacts were formed by spin coating and spray coating methods. Whereas, the dielectric layers were deposited using pulsed rf plasma polymerization. The current-voltage curves showed a bistable current behavior with the presence of hysteresis window. The fabricated NVM memory devices were reprogrammable when the endurance test was performed and stable up to 1×104 s cycles with a distinct ON/OFF ratio of 104. Based on the obtained I-V characteristics, Schottky emission, Poole-Frenkel emission, trapped-charge limited-current and space-charge-limited current were proposed as the dominant conduction mechanisms for the fabricated NVM devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bistable switching
Graphene quantum dot
Hexamethyldisiloxane
Plasma polymerization
Conduction mechanisms

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Thin Film Devices, Sensors and Actuators

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported in-part by the Research University Grant from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia ( GUP-2016-011 ), Nanomite grant: LRGS/2015/UKM-UKM/NANOMITE/04/01 and the Higher Institutions Centre of Excellence (HICOE) ( AKU95 ) from Ministry of Education, Malaysia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cross-sectional structure of the fabricated two-terminal MIM devices (a) reference sample and (b) NVM device. The inset shows an SEM image of the cross-sectional structure for the NVM device. (c) HMDSO thickness versus deposition time. (d) AFM topography of 2min pulsed rf plasma polymerized HMDSO.
Absolute transmittance of the reference sample and the NVM device in the visible region. The inset shows the as-fabricated NVM device.
I–V characteristics of the (a) reference sample and (b) NVM devices on a semi-log scale.
(a) An endurance test of NVM device. The band diagrams illustrated NVM device being (b) programmed, and (c) erased.
The retention stability test of MIM-B NVM device.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of transparent bistable switching memory device using plasmapolymerized hexamethyldisiloxane layers with embedded graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Microengineering and Nanoelectronics, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Bangi 43600, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Applied Physics, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Bangi 43600, Malaysia

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrated the feasibility to fabricate two-terminal non-volatile-memory (NVM) devices using pulsed radio frequency (rf) plasma polymerization and simple solution route. The two-terminal NVM devices were fabricated based on a metal-insulator-metal structure consisting of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) embedded in hexamethyldisiloxane dielectric layers. The charge trapping layer, GQDs and the top contacts were formed by spin coating and spray coating methods. Whereas, the dielectric layers were deposited using pulsed rf plasma polymerization. The current-voltage curves showed a bistable current behavior with the presence of hysteresis window. The fabricated NVM memory devices were reprogrammable when the endurance test was performed and stable up to 1×104 s cycles with a distinct ON/OFF ratio of 104. Based on the obtained I-V characteristics, Schottky emission, Poole-Frenkel emission, trapped-charge limited-current and space-charge-limited current were proposed as the dominant conduction mechanisms for the fabricated NVM devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer bistable switching memory device could be considered as the most promising candidate for large scale, ease of fabrication, high density, cost efficient and transparent non-volatile memories production because it is an essential device to be integrated into most of the electronic logic system to provide or store information for logic operation [1–4]. Hitherto, the polymer non-volatile memory (NVM) devices which are designed as metal-insulator-metal (MIM) structure for resistance switching applications is preferred because of its low cost, two-terminal simple structure, excellence memory characteristics and high scale-down ability [5–9]. Spin-coating technique is a common method used to deposit the polymer solution dielectric layers for the MIM devices [10–12]. However, the pulsed rf plasma polymerization method will be demonstrated as an alternative approach in this study to deposit the dielectric layers on indium-tin-oxide (ITO)-coated glass substrate as it is rarely reported and limited studied by Segui et al. [13] and Busby et al. [14], to the best of our knowledge.

Plasma polymerization deposition is opted as a device fabrication method in this work as it could produce a highly crosslinking structure for various types of monomer at room temperature ease in control the thickness to obtain a smooth surface without a pin hole instead of the common spin-coating technique used to deposit the polymer dielectric layers which requires heat treatment to achieve crosslinking structure and indirectly results in physical damage to plastic substrate which hinder the realization of optically transparent and mechanically flexible electronic devices [15–17]. Besides, this approach is reported as an effective way to form ultrathin film on various substrates to develop new thin gate dielectrics for electronic applications such as humidity sensors, electrical resistors and optical filters. It is also used to form chemical barrier coatings, anticorrosive surfaces, scratch resistance coatings, protective coatings and chemical barrier coatings [18–20] because of its wide selection of monomers, simple processing and low deposition temperature [21,22]. Nevertheless, this useful ability of plasma to polymerize and deposit a solid film from certain organic precursor unintentionally results in a poor control of film deposition and hence restricts its applications. Particularly in the traditional continuous wave discharge where the functional groups of precursor gas are seriously dissociated and few reactive moieties can be retained in the deposited film. Hence, it is desirable to seek for a method to overcome the mentioned problem by tailoring the surface structures of the plasma polymers [20].

Therefore, pulsed radio frequency (rf) plasma polymerization can be employed to alter the density of the monomer functional groups systematically through controlled variation of the plasma on/off duty cycle. Consequently, functional groups present in the monomer can significantly be retained in the polymerized films and yielded higher number of nanoparticles to be attached on the surface of the polymerized films during nanoparticles deposition process [19,23]. In this work, hexamethyldisiloxane (HMDSO) monomer is selected to form the dielectric layers in the MIM structure with embedded GQDs as a charge trapping layer. HMDSO monomer is a non-toxic and environmental friendly monomer with a relatively high ambient vapor temperature. The organosiloxane film also has the potential applications in biocompatible coating for medical implants, permselective membranes, low dielectric constant inter-level dielectrics in the microcircuits and oxygen barrier coating on polymer for packing industry [24–27]. In addition, the high thermal stability of HMDSO is highly desired for electronics applications to maintain the device stability when other components produce heat [28–30].

As aforementioned, GQDs will be used as charge trapping medium sandwiched in HMDSO layers in the fabricated NVM memory devices because of its superior properties in chemical inertness, low toxicity, higher work function than other reported nanoparticles, flexibility and transparency [9,31,32]. In addition, the boundary and quantum confinement effect of GQDs make them a favorable charge trapping medium [33]. The charge trapping medium will be deposited using spin coating method whereas the top contact electrodes of the device could be formed by silver nanowires (AgNWs) using spray-coating techniques without going through the costly and time consuming metal vacuum evaporation process [34].

GQDs in deionized (DI) water (1mg/ml) used in this study were purchased from ACS Material. HMDSO monomer with 98.5% purity (Sigma Aldrich product no: 52630) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used without any further purification. Two metal-insulator-metal (MIM) devices were fabricated with structures of (a) AgNWs/HMDSO/HMDSO/ITO/Glass and (b) AgNWs/HMDSO/GQDs/HMDSO/ITO/Glass, denoted as reference sample and NVM device, respectively, and their schematics are shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b). Inset in Fig. 1(b) presents the cross-sectional image performed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for NVM device. Prior to device fabrication, the ITO/Glass substrate was cleaned by ultra-sonication sequentially in deionized (DI) water, ethanol and DI water for 20min, respectively and dried under nitrogen stream. Pulsed rf plasma polymerization of HMDSO was performed on the 1cm×1cm ITO-coated glass substrates in a custom-built cylindrical glass chamber, sealed by stainless steel top and bottom plate by viton gaskets. The rf power was supplied by RF generator (RFMN-150, 13.56MHz) connected to two copper electrodes with impedance matching [35]. The pulse mode was set to 1.0Vpp with the 2ms period plasma “high power” time in a 50% duty cycle and 1.0ms pulse width in this study. The glass chamber was evacuated by an Edward RV8 pump and the pressure was monitored by a CVM211 stinger vacuum gauge.

Prior to plasma polymerization of HMDSO, the ITO-coated glass substrates were first treated by air plasma for 1min at 10W, 25Pa to remove any possible trace of contaminants. After the air plasma treatment, the chamber pressure was further evacuated to reach a value of 1.3Pa. Then, HMDSO monomer was vaporized into the plasma chamber until it reached a flow rate of 2sccm. Then, the plasma was ignited by a power of 16W with 2ms pulse length at 2min repetition to obtain the first 20nm thick HMDSO layer. Fig. 1(c) estimates the HDMSO thicknesses obtained at different time of deposition. As was expected, the film became thicker with increase in coating time. Fig. 1(d) shows the atomic force microscope surface (AFM) surface topography image of the 2min pulsed rf plasma polymerized layer with a RMS value of 0.422nm. Next, oxygen plasma treatment was performed for 5s to reduce the hydrophobicity of HMDSO layer before spin-coating the GQDs at 2000rpm for 40s. Afterward, the subsequent HMDSO layer was deposited at 16W for 2.5min with the same pulse length to achieve a thickness of 26nm. It is worth to mention that the thinner dielectric layer deposited adjacent to the ITO electrode is to ensure the ease of charge injection from the ITO electrode whereas the thicker dielectric layer is to yield the higher retention stability for the fabricated device. Finally, the sample on a 100°C hot plate was spray-coated with AgNWs in isopropyl alcohol (IPA) with the aid of shadow mask to form 0.5mm diameter circular top electrodes with an air brush pressurized at 0.1MPa.

The current-voltage (I–V) measurements of the fabricated NVM devices were performed at room temperature by using Keithley 4200-SCS semiconductor characterization system. Bias voltages were applied to the top metal electrode with respect to the ITO electrode for all measurements. The transmittance of the NVM devices were studied by Hitachi UV-VIS Spectrophotometer (U-3900H). ZEISS MERLIN field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to obtain the cross-sectional image of the devices. For AFM topography image, an NT-MDT on the active vibration protection tablet TS-150 was used.

Fig. 2 shows the study on transmittance in the visible range from 400 to 800nm transparency for the fabricated MIM devices. The absolute transmittance of the ITO-coated glass varies in the range of 72.8% to 86.2%. On the other hand, the reference sample and the NVM device demonstrated a slight drop of absolute transmittance compared to ITO-coated glass sample which vary from 70.8% to 86.0% and 68.8% to 85.7%, respectively, over the visible spectrum. The inset in Fig. 2 shows the fabricated optically transparent NVM device.

In Fig. 3(b), regions I, II, and III were shown in the I–V curves for NVM device in order to examine the possible dominant conduction mechanisms at different regions of the device. The plotted curve can be expressed as I ∝ Vn and the fitted slope of a double log plot determines the value of the slope, n [33]. It is worth to mention that the occurrence of thermionic emission current conduction at low voltage is a commonly observed mechanism related to thermally generated electrons [36,37]. In region I, electrons could be injected from ITO electrode and transported over the metal-insulator interface via Schottky and Poole-Frenkel (PF) emissions. The obtained experimental data for region I as shown in Fig. 3(b) is found to obey the relation of ln (I / T 2)∝ V 0.5 and ln (I / V)∝ V 0.5, respectively where T is the temperature V is the applied voltage and I is the current., Those two equations are the Schottky and PF emission mechanisms, respectively. When the voltage was applied to the ITO electrode, a more plausible explanation is that the electrons from the ITO electrode are transported across the HMDSO barrier into the GQDs in a two-step process [12]: the electrons move into the HMDSO layer due to thermionic emission via a field-assisted lowering of the barrier at the ITO-HMDSO interface; then, they move again by PF emission based on the field-enhanced thermal emission of electrons trapped in the HMDSO layer into the GQDs. In region II, when the voltage is increased to VON , with a fitted slope value of n≈24.5 (which is n≫2) the transportation mechanism starts to follow the common trapped charge-limited current (TCLC) model [38]. Within this region, the presence of GQDs formed the trap sites start to fill up with electrons. This causes an abrupt increase in current. As all the traps are filled, it enters region III where the transport mechanism switches to trap-free space-charge-limited current (SCLC) with n≈1.8 as predicted by the Lampert–Rose model for SCLC in the bulk [39].

The fabricated NVM device was undergone retention test by switching between ON- or OFF-state under ambient condition for about 1×104 s and the corresponding current was recorded and shown in Fig. 5 . During the test, a 2.5V bias was applied on top of AgNWs electrode to switch the device to the high-current state, and a 0.5 constant bias voltage was applied to examine the stability of the device for 1×104 s. This was important as the 0.5 bias voltage was needed to “extract” (read) the state of the device. The existing state must be maintained throughout the whole “reading process”. After that, a −2.0V bias was applied on top of AgNWs electrode to switch the device to the low-current state, and again a 0.5 constant bias voltage was applied to examine the stability of the device up to 1×104 s. Apparently, it could be observed in Fig. 5 the device is being stable without significant degradation up to 1×104 s with a distinct ON/OFF ratio of 104 times.

In conclusion, we demonstrated the feasibility of using pulsed rf plasma polymerization method to fabricate the NVM devices. The device fabrication process was completed with the combination of solution processable techniques to form the charge trapping layer and top metal contacts. The NVM device constructed in MIM structure with GQDs embedded in HMDSO layers and electrical measurement was performed, and significant I–V memory hysteresis window was obtained. The I–V curve fitting suggests that Schottky emission, PF emission, TCLC and SCLC were identified as the dominant conduction mechanisms through the NVM device. The device shows behavior similar to a flash memory when the endurance test was performed, and it demonstrated high stability up to 1×104 s during the retention test with a distinguishable ON/OFF ratio of 104. Therefore, the fabricated two-terminal NVM device shows promising application to be integrated for the next generation transparent electronic devices.

This research was supported in-part by the Research University Grant from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (GUP-2016-011), Nanomite grant: LRGS/2015/UKM-UKM/NANOMITE/04/01 and the Higher Institutions Centre of Excellence (HICOE) (AKU95) from Ministry of Education, Malaysia.
